verses.commentary: As He went along, He saw a man blind from birth. Congenital blindness. Lifelong darkness. Jesus initiates the encounter. Divine compassion sees need.
Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind? Disciples assume sin causes suffering. Retribution theology. The question reveals limited understanding of God's purposes.

Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him. Jesus rejects simplistic cause. Suffering serves divine glory. Not punishment but platform for revelation.

As long as it is day, we must do the works of Him who sent Me. Night is coming, when no one can work. Limited opportunity. Urgency of mission. Daylight represents Jesus' earthly ministry. Death approaches.
While I am in the world, I am the light of the world. Present tense. Temporary presence. Light imagery from chapter 8 continues. Jesus illuminates spiritually and physically.

After saying this, He spit on the ground, made some mud with the saliva, and put it on the man's eyes. Unusual method. Saliva considered healing in ancient world. Mud recalls creation (Genesis 2:7). Re-creation imagery.

Go, wash in the Pool of Siloam (this word means Sent). So the man went and washed, and came home seeing. Obedience required. Siloam means Sent, pointing to Jesus the Sent One. Faith acts on command. Washing brings sight. Baptismal overtones.

His neighbors and those who had formerly seen him begging asked, Isn't this the same man who used to sit and beg? Identity questioned. Transformation so radical it creates doubt. Change challenges recognition.

Some claimed that he was. Others said, No, he only looks like him. But he himself insisted, I am the man. Confusion persists. The healed man testifies to his identity. Transformation creates testimony.
How then were your eyes opened? The question everyone asks. Explanation demanded. Miracle requires accounting.

The man they call Jesus made some mud and put it on my eyes. He told me to go to Siloam and wash. So I went and washed, and then I could see. Simple testimony. He recounts facts. Jesus identified as agent. Obedience led to sight.
Where is this man? I don't know. He seeks the healer. Cannot find Him. Healing occurred; relationship pending. Physical sight precedes spiritual sight.
They brought to the Pharisees the man who had been blind. Official investigation begins. Religious authorities must examine. Control through interrogation.
Now the day on which Jesus had made the mud and opened the man's eyes was a Sabbath. Problem identified. Healing on Sabbath again. Kneading mud considered work. Legal violation charged.
Therefore the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight. He put mud on my eyes, and I washed, and now I see. Repetition. Same testimony. Consistent account. Truth stands under scrutiny.

Some of the Pharisees said, This man is not from God, for He does not keep the Sabbath. But others asked, How can a sinner perform such signs? So they were divided. Split verdict. Law versus miracle. Some judge by regulation; others by results. Division over Jesus persists.

Then they turned again to the blind man, What have you to say about Him? It was your eyes He opened. The man replied, He is a prophet. Opinion requested. Growing insight. Prophet: one who speaks for God. Theological understanding developing.

They still did not believe that he had been blind and had received his sight until they sent for the man's parents. Stubborn unbelief. Denial of evidence. Parents summoned for verification. Bureaucratic obstruction.
Is this your son? Is this the one you say was born blind? How is it that now he can see? Three questions. Identity, condition, explanation sought. Witnesses interrogated.
We know he is our son, and we know he was born blind. Parents confirm facts. Identity and condition verified. Medical history established.

But how he can see now, or who opened his eyes, we don't know. Ask him. He is of age; he will speak for himself. Strategic evasion. Fear prevents full testimony. Adult son must answer. Parents protect themselves.

His parents said this because they were afraid of the Jewish leaders, who already had decided that anyone who acknowledged that Jesus was the Messiah would be put out of the synagogue. Excommunication threat. Social death. Expelled from covenant community. Economic and relational ruin. Fear silences truth.
That was why his parents said, He is of age; ask him. Explanation for evasion. Terror drives deflection. Self-preservation over son's welfare.

A second time they summoned the man who had been blind. Give glory to God by telling the truth. We know this man is a sinner. Demand for confession. Give glory to God means confess the truth. They've predetermined Jesus is sinner. Verdict before evidence.

He replied, Whether He is a sinner or not, I don't know. One thing I do know. I was blind but now I see! Theological agnosticism. Personal certainty. He cannot judge Jesus' character. But he knows his own experience. Testimony rooted in transformation.
Then they asked him, What did He do to you? How did He open your eyes? Repetitive interrogation. Looking for inconsistency. Searching for grounds to discredit.

He answered, I have told you already and you did not listen. Why do you want to hear it again? Do you want to become His disciples too? Exasperation. Mockery. Bold challenge. He exposes their agenda. Do they truly seek truth or just ammunition?

Then they hurled insults at him and said, You are this fellow's disciple! We are disciples of Moses! Rage erupts. Identity politics. They claim Moses, reject Jesus. False dichotomy. Jesus fulfills Moses.

We know that God spoke to Moses, but as for this fellow, we don't even know where He comes from. Dismissive language. This fellow: contemptuous. Moses validated; Jesus questioned. Origin determines authority in their view.

The man answered, Now that is remarkable! You don't know where He comes from, yet He opened my eyes. Ironic reversal. Religious experts confess ignorance. Uneducated man sees clearly. Power should reveal origin.
We know that God does not listen to sinners. He listens to the godly person who does His will. Standard theology. Sinners lack access. Righteous have God's ear. John 9:31, common belief cited.
Nobody has ever heard of opening the eyes of a man born blind. Unprecedented miracle. No historical parallel. Unique sign. Isaiah 35:5 promised Messiah would do this.
If this man were not from God, He could do nothing. Logical conclusion. Power indicates divine origin. Miracle validates mission. The beggar becomes theologian.

To this they replied, You were steeped in sin at birth; how dare you lecture us! And they threw him out. Ad hominem attack. Congenital blindness blamed on sin. How dare the unlearned teach the scholars? Expulsion: excommunication fulfilled. He pays the cost his parents feared.

Jesus heard that they had thrown him out, and when He found him, He said, Do you believe in the Son of Man? Jesus seeks the exiled. Divine initiative. Son of Man: Daniel 7:13, Messianic title. Faith question posed.
Who is He, sir? Tell me so that I may believe in Him. Willingness to believe. Openness. He lacks information, not disposition. Faith ready, awaits object.

Jesus said, You have now seen Him; in fact, He is the One speaking with you. Revelation. First time he physically sees Jesus (healed eyes now functional). Identity disclosed. The Speaker is the Messiah.
Then the man said, Lord, I believe, and he worshiped Him. Confession and worship. Kyrios (κύριος), Lord. Worship appropriate only for God. Physical and spiritual sight achieved. Full conversion.

Jesus said, For judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind. Ironic reversal. Spiritually blind receive sight. Physically seeing become spiritually blind. Jesus exposes and reverses. Judgment through revelation.
Some Pharisees who were with Him heard Him say this and asked, What? Are we blind too? Offended question. Self-assessment: we see. Implicit claim to spiritual sight.

Jesus said, If you were blind, you would not be guilty of sin; but now that you claim you can see, your guilt remains. Presumed sight brings guilt. Acknowledged blindness allows healing. Their claim to see prevents receiving sight. Pride blocks grace. Judgment rests on self-assessment.
